reason: Both answers correctly diagnose the Goodhart's law dynamic (incentive selecting for low-intent signups/fraud), note activated users are likely flat despite doubled signups, and recommend the core fix (pay on activation, not signup) plus fraud/segmentation considerations. A adds extra nuances (cash vs. credit incentive design, an uncertainty flag about checking absolute activation counts rather than just the rate, capping referrals). B is more concise and explicitly calls out comparing referred vs. organic cohorts to rule out confounds (e.g., a broken funnel unrelated to the referral program), a sharp diagnostic point. Both are correct, well-organized, and actionable with no disqualifying errors — differences are minor tradeoffs of extra depth (A) versus concision plus one added diagnostic angle (B).
